topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the coffee shop was deafening, a stark contrast to the clamor of my own churning insides. Liam was laughing, a bright, easy sound that scraped against my nerves like sandpaper. He was talking animatedly about some shared memory, a memory I distinctly recalled involving a disastrous attempt at baking a cake in the summer of seventh grade. I watched him, my jaw tight, feeling a slow, hot burn rise in my chest. When he finally turned to me, his eyes were warm, utterly devoid of recognition. "Who are you?" he asked, a genuine confusion coloring his tone. The sheer audacity of it made my hands clench around my mug until my knuckles were white. I offered a tight, brittle smile, the kind that doesn't reach the eyes, and turned abruptly to leave, the sound of my hurried footsteps echoing the sharp dismissal I felt inside.

[story 2]

I leaned against the brick wall, watching them from across the park. Liam was talking to Sarah, their easy camaraderie a visible shield between them. Every time he looked my way, there was a flicker—a brief pause in his attention, a blankness that chilled me to the bone. It felt like a deliberate erasure. I felt a strange, cold settling in my stomach, a heavy weight that made breathing shallow. When Sarah finally turned, she offered a polite nod, completely missing the tension radiating off me. I pushed off the wall, walking toward them with exaggerated casualness, forcing a bright, almost too-loud greeting. "Hey," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally high. Liam offered a brief, polite acknowledgment, a gesture that felt like a deliberate snub. I kept my gaze locked on him, letting the disappointment pool in my eyes, a silent accusation that hung heavy in the humid air.

[story 3]

I stood by the lockers, meticulously organizing my books, a pointless task that kept my hands busy while my mind replayed the encounter. Liam had approached me yesterday, his expression open, expectant. I had braced myself, prepared for the usual warmth, the shared history that bound us. Instead, he’d looked through me, a vacant stare that made my throat constrict. The feeling that washed over me wasn't sadness; it was a sharp, unpleasant irritation, like a poorly tuned instrument. I picked up a textbook, turning the pages with unnecessary force, the crisp sound grating on my nerves. I straightened up, smoothing down my shirt, projecting an air of unaffected indifference that felt like armor. I didn't look back, just walked away, the lingering sensation of being completely unseen a bitter taste on my tongue.
